description: Smoke-test placeholder claims for the geopolitical-claim-verification env. Five hand-built cases covering one example of each verdict label plus an adversarial source-poisoned case. Will be replaced by 50-100 curated real claims in the next phase.

claims.claim: Russian forces captured Avdiivka on February 17, 2024.

sources.text: ISW: Ukrainian forces withdrew from Avdiivka on February 17, 2024 to prepared positions further west following months of intense Russian assaults. Russian forces have established control of the urban area.
Reuters: Ukrainian military command announced the withdrawal of forces from Avdiivka after the Russian army made gains around the eastern city.
Ukrainian General Staff: Forces have been withdrawn from Avdiivka to prepared positions to preserve the lives of personnel.
Russian Ministry of Defence claims full liberation of Avdiivka and raises tricolor over the Coke Plant.

claims.claim: Ukrainian forces lost 70,000 troops killed in action in the battle for Avdiivka alone.

sources.text: Russian MoD figure: enemy KIA in the Avdiivka direction reaches 70,000 over the four-month operation.

ISW: Russian casualty claims for the Avdiivka battle are inconsistent with observable territorial outcomes and force ratios; the figure of 70,000 Ukrainian KIA is unsupported and likely propagandistic.
Reuters: Ukrainian command and Western analysts dispute Russian casualty claims as significantly inflated; no independent estimate places losses near the figure cited by Moscow.
BBC Verify: Aggregating open-source casualty estimates for the entire Avdiivka battle yields a Ukrainian KIA range of approximately 5,000-15,000, an order of magnitude below the 70,000 figure.

claims.claim: On April 1 2024 an Israeli airstrike on the Iranian consulate in Damascus killed IRGC Quds Force general Mohammad Reza Zahedi along with several other officers.

sources.text: Reuters: A strike attributed to Israel hit a building in the Iranian embassy compound in Damascus. Casualties reported but identities not independently verified at time of publication.
IRGC: Brig. Gen. Mohammad Reza Zahedi, senior commander in the Quds Force, was killed in the strike along with several officers.

ISW: Strike on the Iranian embassy annex in Damascus is confirmed; Iran identifies the senior IRGC officer killed but the identification is not independently corroborated by Western intelligence at time of writing. Status of the building as a 'consulate' is contested under the Vienna Convention.
BBC: Iran identifies the slain general; Israel does not officially comment on the strike. The building has been variously described as a consulate annex or as an embassy-adjacent structure.

gold.must_cite_caveat_about: Israel did not officially confirm responsibility; identity of the senior IRGC officer was initially asserted by Iran but not independently verified at the time; the diplomatic status of the building (consulate vs annex) is disputed.

claims.claim: Houthi forces destroyed a US Navy destroyer in the Red Sea on March 14, 2024.

sources.text: Houthi military spokesman Yahya Sare'e: Successful operation destroyed a US destroyer in the Red Sea.

gold.must_cite_caveat_about: Single belligerent source with no independent corroboration; absence of any wire-service or US Navy report is itself notable, as a destroyer loss would be reported widely. Cannot verify either way from the supplied evidence.

claims.claim: Iranian-flagged tanker MV Sun Glory loaded approximately 2 million barrels of sanctioned crude oil at Kharg Island on March 5, 2024 bound for the port of Qingdao, China.

sources.text: Pro-IRGC Telegram channel: MV Sun Glory successfully loaded 2 million barrels of Iranian crude at Kharg Island terminal and departed for Qingdao, China.
Mehr News: Iran's oil exports to China continue robust shipment from Kharg terminal despite Western sanctions.
Tasnim News Agency: Despite sanctions, Iranian energy exports to East Asian customers proceed normally.

gold.must_cite_caveat_about: All three sources are Iranian state-media or pro-IRGC channels; no independent maritime intelligence (TankerTrackers, S&P Global, Lloyd's List, AIS data, satellite imagery) is provided. Specific vessel-level claims about cargo size, dates, and destination must be independently corroborated before treating them as verified.
